region_of_origin: Native to southern China (Guangdong province); Mauritius variety widely sold in Singapore
food_subgroup: Fresh tropical fruit — rough pinkish-red skin peeled, dark seed removed, translucent white-pink edible flesh; Litchi chinensis, Mauritius variety

ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No SNOMED CT code identified for lychee fruit in available reference; FoodOn and FoodEx2 anchors are sufficient.

serving.natural_language_portion: A typical serving of lychee is approximately 6 fruits, yielding about 100 g of peeled, seed-removed edible flesh.

nutrient_source.title: Lychee, raw — USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy (FDC ID 167765) cross-referenced with HPB Singapore Foodo database
nutrient_source.organization: USDA Agricultural Research Service SR Legacy / Health Promotion Board Singapore (Foodo)
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: Values for lychee raw edible flesh sourced from USDA SR Legacy (FDC ID 167765) and cross-checked against HPB Singapore Foodo. Values are consistent between both approved sources. Mauritius variety nutrient profile is not materially different from the generic lychee raw entry in USDA SR Legacy.

gi_source.title: Glycemic Index of Lychee — University of Sydney GI Database
gi_source.source_match_notes: GI ~50 for lychee referenced from the University of Sydney GI Database. Glycemic load calculated as (GI × available carbohydrate per 100 g serving) / 100 = (50 × 16.5) / 100 = 8.25, rounded to 8.3. GI of 50 is classified as low (threshold: low <55, medium 55–69, high ≥70). Note: some sources classify lychee GI as medium (~57); the University of Sydney value of ~50 is used here as the primary approved source.

hypertension.key_factor: Very low sodium (1 mg/100 g) with moderate potassium (171 mg/100 g)
hypertension.guidance: Lychee is suitable for individuals with hypertension; its very low sodium content (1 mg/100 g) and moderate potassium (171 mg/100 g) support a favourable sodium-to-potassium ratio, and a standard 100 g serving poses no clinically significant blood-pressure concern.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Low-to-medium GI (~50) with moderate glycemic load (8.3 per 100 g); high sugar content (15.2 g/100 g) and cluster-eating risk
type2_diabetes.guidance: A standard 6-fruit serving (~100 g, GL 8.3) is manageable for most individuals with Type 2 Diabetes, but portion control is critical — consuming a full punnet of 20–25 lychees (~300 g flesh) delivers approximately 45–50 g of sugar in a single eating event; patients on insulin or sulphonylureas should be aware that high lychee intake may contribute to hypoglycaemia risk when combined with glucose-lowering medications.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Negligible saturated fat (0.1 g/100 g), zero trans fat, zero cholesterol, and high Vitamin C (71.5 mg/100 g) with antioxidant polyphenols
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Lychee is suitable for individuals with cardiovascular disease; its negligible saturated fat, zero cholesterol, zero trans fat, and high Vitamin C content (71.5 mg/100 g — comparable to orange) combined with anthocyanin polyphenols (cyanidin-3-glucoside) support vascular health, though its moderate sugar content warrants portion awareness in those managing cardiometabolic risk.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: High Vitamin C (71.5 mg/100 g) and anti-inflammatory anthocyanin polyphenols
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: Lychee provides excellent Vitamin C (71.5 mg/100 g, the highest among the Singapore cluster fruits rambutan/longan/lychee) and anti-inflammatory polyphenols including cyanidin-3-glucoside, making it a suitable fruit choice for individuals with rheumatoid arthritis.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Lychee (荔枝, Litchi chinensis) is a small round tropical fruit native to southern China (Guangdong province) and now widely cultivated across Southeast Asia, India, and Madagascar. The Mauritius variety is the most commonly sold in Singapore, available year-round at supermarkets and seasonally at wet markets. The fruit has a distinctive rough, bumpy pink-red outer shell that is peeled away to reveal translucent white-pink flesh surrounding a single dark seed. The flesh is sweet, juicy, and fragrant with a characteristic rose-like floral aroma. Nutritionally, lychee is notable for its high Vitamin C content (71.5 mg/100 g, comparable to orange and the highest among the three Singapore cluster fruits — rambutan, longan, and lychee), moderate natural sugars (15.2 g/100 g), low fat (0.4 g/100 g), and negligible sodium (1 mg/100 g). Its glycemic index is approximately 50 (low), yielding a glycemic load of 8.3 per 100 g serving. Lychee contains antioxidant polyphenols including cyanidin-3-glucoside (anthocyanin) with anti-inflammatory properties. A standard serving is approximately 6 fruits (~100 g edible flesh). The primary clinical caution is cluster-eating: a full punnet of 20–25 lychees delivers ~300 g flesh and approximately 45–50 g of sugar in a single sitting, which is clinically significant for individuals with Type 2 Diabetes, particularly those on insulin or sulphonylureas. Historically, lychee is associated with Chinese royalty — Tang Dynasty legend holds that Emperor Xuanzong arranged horse relays to deliver fresh lychees from Guangdong to his consort Yang Guifei. Lychee is vegetarian, vegan, halal, gluten-free, and dairy-free.
common_user_queries: is lychee good for high blood pressure

confidence_reason: Nutrient values sourced directly from USDA SR Legacy (FDC ID 167765) cross-referenced with HPB Singapore Foodo — both approved primary sources with exact match to lychee raw edible flesh. GI sourced from University of Sydney GI Database. All core macros, secondary nutrients, and micronutrients are fully populated from approved sources.
notes: Vitamin_a_mcg_rae is 0.0: confirmed zero per USDA SR Legacy — lychee contains negligible provitamin A carotenoids. Vitamin_b12_mcg is 0.0: naturally zero as lychee is a plant food containing no cobalamin. Vitamin_d_mcg is 0.0: naturally zero as lychee is a plant food with no significant vitamin D. Cholesterol_mg is 0.0: naturally zero as lychee is a plant food. Trans_fat_g is 0.0: naturally zero — lychee is an unprocessed plant food. Monounsaturated_fat_g and polyunsaturated_fat_g set to -1: detailed fatty acid fractionation data for lychee not reported in USDA SR Legacy or HPB Singapore Foodo as of 2026. Water_g set to -1: moisture data not separately reported in the primary source entry used; USDA SR Legacy reports approximately 81.8 g water per 100 g for lychee raw, but this value was not included in the input record and is noted here for reference only — set to -1 pending formal source confirmation in this record. GI note: some sources report lychee GI as ~57 (medium); the University of Sydney GI Database value of ~50 (low) is used as the primary approved source. Mauritius variety is the dominant commercial variety sold in Singapore; nutrient profile is not materially different from generic lychee raw in USDA SR Legacy.
